summary: O'Neil examines the recent food stamp cuts in America and questions whether the savings actually make economic sense when accounting for downstream healthcare costs. She draws on personal experience managing blood sugar to illustrate how expensive healthy eating is, noting that cheap food is inherently bad for metabolic health. The post argues that forcing poor people onto cheaper, unhealthier diets will likely increase diabetes diagnoses and worsen existing cases. With diabetes care costs already rising 40% over five years to $245 billion, she suggests the billions saved on food stamps may be dwarfed by increased medical costs. She frames this as both a moral failing and potentially a fiscal miscalculation.
key_points: Food stamp cuts have overwhelmed food pantries and forced diabetic recipients to choose between appropriate food and cheaper unhealthy alternatives
Healthy eating requires real money - cheap calories come from foods that destroy blood sugar control
The scale of potential costs is comparable: billions in food stamp cuts versus a diabetes care industry already at $245 billion and growing 40% every five years
The analysis should consider not just immediate savings but the long-term cost of creating new diabetics and worsening existing cases
Policy decisions that seem fiscally conservative may actually be penny-wise and pound-foolish when externalities are included
money_quotes: Eating well in this country is expensive, and I've had to spend real money on food here in New York City to avoid sugary and fast carb-laden food.
Because here's the thing, and it's not a secret: shitty food is cheap.
If I need to buy lots of food (read: calories) for a small amount of money, I can do it easily, but it will be hell for my blood sugar control.
I'm guessing I'd be a full-blown diabetic by now if I were poor and on food stamps.

How much money are we really saving by decreasing the food stamp allowance in this country, if we consider how many more people will be diagnosed diabetic as a result of the decreased quality of their diet?
Never mind in a moral context – I'm definitely of the mind that people should be able to eat – I'm wondering if the food stamp cuts make sense in a dollars and cents context.
key_insight: Food stamp cuts may cost more in downstream diabetes care than they save, making them not just morally questionable but potentially fiscally irrational.
